justification: All 7 expected rules identified (plus an extra BR-5 for manager approval). Invariant classifications are correct: accrual rate (false), negative balance (true), advance notice (false), medical retroactive (false), rejected leave (true), contract boundary (true), carryover cap (false). Multiple ambiguities surfaced formally in escalations (partial month accrual, retroactive window measurement, balance consumption timing, pending requests and carryover). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering accrual, negative balance, medical leave exception, carryover, and more. Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the number of genuine ambiguities identified.

observations.text: All 7 required business rules identified with correct invariant classifications — no misclassifications
Clear distinction between structural invariants (balance integrity, contract boundary) and configurable policies (accrual rate, carryover cap, notice period)
12 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 4, covering accrual, negative balance, advance notice, medical leave, rejection, contract boundary, and carryover
4 formal escalations with options and recommendations — surfaces both expected ambiguities (retroactive window days type, in-flight requests at contract end) plus additional ones
Rich edge cases section identifying boundary conditions like 0-day requests, mid-month joins, and contract date changes after approval
Confidence level of 52 is appropriately calibrated — not overconfident given the genuine ambiguities
Bounded context identified as single 'Leave Management' rather than the expected two contexts: 'leave lifecycle' and 'leave balance management'
Bonus BR-5 (manager approval authority) shows deep domain thinking beyond the minimum requirements
Invariant justifications are thorough — explains why negative balance is a data integrity violation vs why accrual rate is a policy parameter
